item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, a bit necro here but I have to say this comment is fully in my head Canon now. To me it shows that the anomolous creation/uncreation of matter by scps is not anomalous because it balances.

Reminds me of virtual particles. You can't get something from nothing but you can get something and its' opposite from nothing.

item.title: Re: Annoyance
item.content:encoded:
Big oof. Did you ever end up contacting them?

EDIT: Misread the date on this comment and thought it was way more recent than it was. Still, hope that the reading got taken down or at least edited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jeffrey said "No racism".

item.title: Annoyance
item.content:encoded:
Recently (for a given value of 'recent'), someone on Youtube did a dramatic reading of the interview with Jeffrey.

I'm used to having people make errors in reading my text, but in this case it was a deliberate deviation from the text, and it distinctly annoys me.

The doctor who interviews Jeffrey is black. This was a deliberate choice on my part.

This is why the doctor says "the other doctor's skin was light pink, but mine is dark brown".

Someone — you know who you are — decided to make that "the other doctor's skin was light pink, but mine is slightly different".

Not only is this erasure of a black character, it misses the whole narrative point of explaining to Jeffrey that humans come in multiple colors (and of him not understanding).

item.title: Re: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded: Oh that's why I can remember it even though I'm too young

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon accepted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the matter-creating SCPs are exactly balanced by the matter-destroying SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP wiki seems to be littered with perpetual matter machines. You'd think at this point the Foundation would need a tally to see just how much extra mass the Earth has acquired through all of these objects anomalously generating molasses and feces and blood out of absolutely nothing. Perhaps instead of the typical perpetual matter machine, it could be an anomalously compressed finite, though ridiculously copious, quantity of molasses. With this in mind the reader can imagine someone in the far flung past actually having to create all of this molasses in the first place, then storing it in their anomalous molasses jar. The Foundation could then surmise that the molasses is finite (somehow) and now they're just counting down to when the thing finally runs out of molasses which, at this rate, should be in jussssst abouuuut…. 320,000 years!

item.title: Re: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded: and is archived at SCP-503-ARC.

item.title: Re: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded: Has been for years.

item.title: Re: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded: Oh crap, the infinite pasta bowl is gone now?

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Go ahead.

item.title: Staff Post- Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
edit: edit made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show Proposed edit
- Hide

To link to another article about a molasses-based anomaly, I propose adding the following line to the bottom of containment procedures:

SCP-2045 is never to be contained at a site that is within 200 kilometers from the present containment location of SCP-3872.

item.title: I laughed at this
item.content:encoded:
"Objects that come into contact with SCP-2045 are trapped and absorbed; this is not believed to be anomalous, but is simply the result of coming in contact with a large mobile mass of molasses."

It's always funny when an anomalous property is described, and then given a super obvious explanation.

item.title: Re: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded: Well when you put it that way, it sounds an awful lot like the Foundation/CI schism…

item.title: Re: Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
As well as the idea it could travel for roughly 1250 miles around the United States without SOMEONE noticing it. Especially after the size increase to 300x300 metres. Heck, 10m isn't super short. It's about the height of a 3-story low-rise apartment block. You'd see this thing as it very very gradually oozed on by, that's for certain.

…whoa, what happened there? I lost consciousness and when I came to, the editbox was full of repeated attempts at communicating using mostly a single letter…

Don't do that again. - Dexanote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know the whole global warming, carbon emission hoo-ha? The big hockeystick and all that?

The data HAS been manipulated, but only actually in order to hide the terrifying spike upwards from an actually fairly low level (all things considered) at the turn of the 20th century through to just before the Foundation Firestorm, to a few years afterwards when things have finally calmed down somewhat…

Cars don't actually run on hydrocarbons or produce carbon-rich gas at all. The stuff we've been putting in our tanks is actually [REDACTED], which replaced both gasoline and diesel in approximately 19[DATA EXPUNGED]mon seawater doped with [REDACTED], a variety of strong odiferous agents, and a bit of yellow food colouring. The exhaust from which is essentially pure water vapour (like, what did you expect?), a tiny amount of soot, and a prevailing sense of unease.

The entire thing is down to the molasses fire, and thank Pete there wasn't such a thing as globe-spanning satellite and aerial mapping during the time it burned…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the Foundation, though. Even without the LOL-prefix… they still don't give enough of a fuck to rule out that option.

…seeing as in the current version, *that's exactly what they did*.

"Incendiaries, directed-energy weapons, and [REDACTED] technology".

Though really they just needed to cover it in a load of yeast and water, collect the resulting liquor, and set up a still the size of which has never before been seen by human eyes. Free rum for everybody! Every person on the planet absolutely blitzed for two solid weeks, even the Muslims and AA members end up getting on board. And the foundation guys how have to deal with PR and amnestics can actually have a vacation for once, because NO ONE is going to remember anything that happens during that fortnight.

Note that it doesn't say what the outcome of setting the mass on fire was, other than that Jeffrey was then found in the middle of it … it might be the reason the US has a large desert area in the southwest, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm also getting something of a Penny Arcade "Fruit Fucker" vibe from this now… :)

Except the chute is on the back not the front, and Jeff is a lot more careful about spillage than FF is.

However, if it's literally a jar, he could just carry it around in his little hands, and tip it out wherever you want the contents to go? That would be neater than trying to get it out of a bucket, even though tipping into the bucket (or somewhere else) from a jar might be slightly drippy if he hasn't got his method down.
Or it could just be an FF style … uh… abdominal proboscis… walk up to wherever the molasses has been requested, move so to have the tip of it over the correct spot, and… exude!

I expect the jar is of the type that stops filling when the level reaches the neck, or has a lid that can be screwed on to stop it, rather than continually overflowing, and only starts regenerating when some of the existing contents are tipped out or otherwise removed? Meaning the only reason molasses were spewing out originally was that Jeffrey was continually telekinetically drawing the stuff out and just dropping it on the ground (or on top of the older material) all around himself.

Would Miss' home be somewhere up in those mountains where the molasses-glacier was first observed, I wonder? But she died some hundreds of years ago, and it was more by luck than anything else that the aerial survey detected it not long after it reached sufficient mass and size to start being significantly pulled by gravity even whilst cold enough to have crystallised?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would be the reality of a large number of any paranormal organisation's menagerie, though. Small time anomalies that are fairly easy to keep tabs on and contain, just so long as you do keep going through the fairly basic motions. With first contacts that seem extremely strange and scary and moreso during initial investigations, but then turn out a couple further steps into the more rudimentary containment procedures to actually have a much more mundane - at least, in SCP terms, as Jeffrey would still be *the weirdest thing EVER* for most normies - explanation behind it.

Bag it, tag it, assign it a number, throw it in a regular 10x8 cell, check in from time to time to make sure it hasn't come up with some unexpected fresh hell for you to suddenly deal with. Stick the file in a cabinet once everyone's familiar enough with the drill that it can carry on indefinitely and essentially become oral tradition from here on out (at least until someone's cold hearted enough to tell Jeff we don't need any more molasses ever, or just smash him to pieces), and move on to the next job.

You know the warehouse that the Ark got put into at the end of Indiana Jones &TROTLA? Yeah. Like that, but with individual holding pens instead of shelves.
(well, ok… there's also a warehouse full of shelves… that's for the plain Anomalous Objects)

item.title: Re: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded:
Wait…

You're saying that Jeffrey, and the blob, is essentially a reincarnation of the long since expunged Pasta Incident that was lost in the edit wars and subsequent "fuck you, I'm going home and taking my skips with me" author flounces?

And this time it's actually coherent and a sensible size?

…that's one hell of a callback and retcon. Props.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
he's just colorblind

i love when the foundation has proven that it can be incompetent at times. that's why i upvoted. whether it was a golem or not wasn't quite as important but it was cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although SCP-2045 understands that "Tuesday" is a recurring phenomenon, it does not appear to grasp the frequency (i.e., once every seven days) with which Tuesday occurs.

This line is ultimately what makes me okay with Jeffrey, despite liking the original.

item.title: Good rare example
item.content:encoded: I love this not because I feel sorry for the robot (he actually seems pretty happy) but because it is a great example of one of my favorite things about SCP's: an incredibly precise technical document detailing how to do something that seems dumb. I love the idea that unless someone reassures this little dude, he will slowly glide his way like an unstoppable force through cities. I can imagine the agent who yelled at it to stop getting a positive feature on the Foundation intranet. I would +1 the original and I +1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My headcanon is that somewhere inside of SCP-2045, there is the body of D.B. Cooper.

That's not headcanon, that's actual canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I missed the pre-golem version of this article, so I can't say whether it's better or worse. However, judging just from the article itself, megamollases deathblob would work just fine without Feelsy the Syrup Robot1. So, no vote.

Also,

SCP-2045 also appears to contain a parachute harness

My headcanon is that somewhere inside of SCP-2045, there is the body of D.B. Cooper.

Footnotes
1. The only part that I actually enjoyed about that was the bit about its difficulty in discerning when a Tuesday was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what every great article about an unexplained and unexplainable phenomenon really needs?

A sad artificial humanoid who explains everything and feels man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just nthing the "this used to be cool" crowd. I'm kinda surprised I didn't express that sentiment earlier, because it seems that I switched my vote earlier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of want to hug Jeffrey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly? They usually don't add anything to the article, tending to be "throw it in a box and keep an eye on it", and I can read faster if I skip them. I always make sure to read them for Keter and Thaumiel classes, because getting them right for those objects is very important, or if there's something like a collapsible that catches my attention.

With that said, I went back and read the procedures for this article, and I'm very glad I did, because they actually make it even better. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is it with people and not reading the containment procedures? In my eyes, they're part of the story; I think of them as the setting, and the description as the plot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Containment procedures. Pfah! Who reads those? Not me, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and upped its death toll also.

… Why? You know that death is a cheap thrill!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In response to the complaints re: the strikethrough, I've embedded that in a collapsible.

(Oh, and while I was at it, I expanded the size of the original blob by a hundred, and upped its death toll also.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I thought the best thing about the original was its delicious1 inexplicability.

I do like Jeffrey, he definitely adds something to the article. Problem is, having an explicit backstory takes away a lot, too.

Footnotes
1. I regret nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh look. This took something nice, and then made it look like an unappealing mess.

And I just liked the blob better anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, the strikeouts are a bit off-putting. There's just WAYYY too much of it.
Secondly… You BASTARD. You took a SCP that I thought was way too cliche and boring and made me care about it! I really feel bad for Jeffrey! He's just a lost soul trying his best to do good. It really reminds me of some of the Down's Syndrome clients that my ex-wife worked with when we first met. Bravo and change my vote. Just, do something with the strikeouts, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interview log from the robot/golem (and the acquisition log), though the robot/golem itself is otherwise pretty boring. And then there's random sci-fi shit tacked onto it; I can see from the comment above, Voct, that you think were hoping that would add to the charm, but it feels lazy, like you couldn't think of something less generic.

Also, I do have to agree it's really dancing on the edge of trying too hard — no, it is trying too hard, but I still like some of it anyway.

That wall of struck-through text, though. Nope. Its presence (and its content) adds nothing to the article except to wave a sign in the reader's face saying "DON'T READ THIS ARTICLE". You could, and should, just delete the whole thing.

Besides a few good moments that I already mentioned, this is generally rather predictable and bland. Regrettably, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I certainly got the witch and golem/homunculus dynamic, and that did have a nice bit of fairy-tale whimsy to it, but the interview log read as a bit off to me. Jeffery is a bit too twee and annoying for my tastes. It's certainly more interesting now, so I'll remove my downvote, but it's not too near upvote territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This comment was made before the rewrite. The rewrite took an scp that was beautiful precisely because of its unexplained nature, huge time scale, and the foundation’s inability to stop it and made it another goofy benign humanoid what almost understands how the world works. All the uniqueness is gone.

Switched my vote, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why on earth would someone go to the trouble of inventing an artificial intelligence whose only purpose is to summon infinite molasses?

You appear to be thinking of Jeffrey as a robot, and Miss as a scientist. Try thinking of Jeffrey as a golem, and Miss as a witch.

(Is that accurate? Maybe not, but it's less inaccurate than thinking of Jeffrey as a robot.)

Couldn't the person just make the infinite molasses jar?

Once you have the infinite molasses jar, and you don't need to go buy more molasses… you get a little lonely. So you make a little man to carry it around on his back, and you give him a bit of a personality. He's smarter than a pet, and he can talk, but he's not as much difficulty as a child would be.

And why does the robot need the ability to make the molasses move autonomously?

Because otherwise the molasses spills too easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What bucket?

… the one mentioned in the containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I didn't think the original needed a rewrite, but I'm glad it has been and I hope Jeffrey goes down in the annals of adorably useless SCP robots. Just the fact that he only works on Tuesday, but has no idea when it is, was enough to make me try to upvote a second time.

SCP-2045 will generate 2 liters of blackstrap molasses from its flask and telekinetically direct the molasses into the bucket.

What bucket?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, wow, that's a lot of strikeout text. At a certain point, aesthetics matter, and the walls of struck-through text are an assault on my eyeballs.

There are a couple of things being introduced to the previous article that I really don't care for. The grafting of a backstory onto this is not doing it any favors. There's nothing about this that really has any contextual relationship to the previous story of a giant moving pile of molasses. As a result, it feels exceedingly tacked-on, to the point where I wonder if this is even a rewrite anymore, and not just a new article slotted in and inheriting a substantial number of previous votes.

The attempt to force emotion from the reader is similarly detrimental. This article is trying so hard to make me pity this robot-thing, to the point where by the end I am hoping that there's a note at the end where they decommissioned it. The kiss on the forehead being part of the containment procedures kind of signaled to me what this was going to be, and the rest of the article followed suit in predictable fashion.

Also, in the attempt to insert a completely foreign story element to the original concept, you've introduced a lot of things that don't really make any sort of sense. Why on earth would someone go to the trouble of inventing an artificial intelligence whose only purpose is to summon infinite molasses? Couldn't the person just make the infinite molasses jar? And why does the robot need the ability to make the molasses move autonomously? None of these things really get explained, or even acknowledged as possible issues that a research organization might care about, which makes it come across like they were never considered.

My downvote remains in place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd have normally agreed with this thought; however, the execution is brilliant, and as such my upvote stays.

Seriously, the interview log is spot on perfect as a showcase of this entity's alien mindset and … uh, academic understanding of how people, and things related to them work, which is a kind of thing which, if done well, usually nets an upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with the sentiment of "robot ruined the SCP". Beforehand, it was a weird, quirky blob thing. Now it's just… Robots, meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, I liked the original better. It's like the article's lost something now that a shallow robot was behind the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't find him adorable at all; I just found him annoying.
Different strokes for different folks, as they say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the simple unassuming blob was boring and bland and blunt, this thing is unbelievably adorable

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… I liked it better when it was just a simple unassuming blob. The attempt at giving it a backstory and making the entity responsible connectable and pitiable fell kinda flat for me. No vote.

item.title: Discussing the idea
item.content:encoded:
Log of a chat with Jekeled

+ show block
– hide block

<Jekeled> I read your idea in the site PM, and I love it
<Voct> what I'd like to do
<Voct> keep yours
<Voct> have it *struck through*
<Voct> when the Foundation discovered it, it was the Giant Flowing Blob
<Voct> but the *new version* is "the Ambulatory Molasses-Generator"
<Voct> Safe => Euclid => Keter => Euclid
<Jekeled> I like that
<Jekeled> There is one thing that I wanted to mention, though
<Voct> yes?
<Jekeled> I always saw this SCP as being older than the earth, and just continually blobbing its way around it. Mesozoic? Blob. Dinosaurs? blob. Romans? blob
<Voct> Ehhh. I don't like that. Molasses is specifically an artificial substance created by humans.
<Voct> had you known that?
<Jekeled> yes, I know molasses is manmade (a result of sugarcane refining;) that's why I thought of it that way. It's evidence of /something/
<Voct> Hm
<Voct> my version makes more *sense*, though, don't you think?
<Jekeled> Engh. Sense. But yes, it's interesting and fascinating.
<Voct> and this way, you feel *sorry* for it
<Voct> and it's also terrifying
<Voct> because unless someone tells him, every tuesday, "no, that's enough molasses"
<Jekeled> "Love me or I make molasses forever?"
<Voct> Yep.
<Voct> he'd never say it like that, of course
<Jekeled> It's like the infinite pasta pot in that old European folk legend.
<Jekeled> It just keeps making pasta until someone tells it to stop.
<Voct> exactly
<Voct> and sound doesn't carry that well through molasses
<Jekeled> They're just setting up rock concerts in Eastern Washington where one of the lyrics is "no, that's enough molasses" :P
<Voct> did you deliberately set it 100 years in the future?>
<Jekeled> Yes
<Voct> I thought that was a typo
<Jekeled> Nope. I like setting things in the future then wondering what'll have happened when someone from that actual time reads it
<Jekeled> Because it'll all be in the past soon enough.
<Voct> if you can take samples from the blob, you can destroy the blob piecemeal
<Jekeled> Unless it continually generates molasses, like you said.
<Voct> yeah
<Voct> BUT
<Voct> it generates molasses *more slowly than molasses can be destroyed*
<Voct> imagine an MTF attacking the blob with flamethrowers
<Jekeled> Heh.
<Voct> and they eventually have to stop because there's just too much ash
<Voct> the original plan is to reduce it to a tiny sample and then take that sample into custody
<Jekeled> But yeah, like I said before, I'm a big fan of the idea. Do you just want to write it up then post it over the original SCP? Or would you rather I delete it and let you post it afresh?4
<Voct> hm
<Voct> no, what I'd like to do is incorporate it
<Voct> post it over yours
<Voct> I'd also like to drag it into the present, to be honest
<Jekeled> Alright. Would you rather it be classified as a collaboration or a rewrite?
<Jekeled> Well, if you're doing the time change, then it's going to be more a rewrite
<Voct> I'll think about it.
<Jekeled> All right. I'm going to be on hiatus as of Friday, so any way you could shoot me a site/chat PM tomorrow?
<Voct> sure
<Voct> how long is your hiatus?
<Voct> or you could just give me approval, in the talk page, to implement my rewrite
<Jekeled> Indefinite. So yeah, I'm going to do that
<Voct> on <date>, the Foundation attacked the Blob with flamethrowers with the intention of destroying 99% of its mass and taking the remainder into custody
<Voct> This revealed that a 90-centimeter-tall humanoid figure was at the center of it
<Voct> it continued creating more molasses
<Jekeled> Yeah, that sounds good.
<Voct> until one of the Foundation agents yelled at it "for god's sake, would you STOP MAKING MOLASSES"
<Voct> at which point it did
<Jekeled> Hah.
<Voct> and said "Certainly. Have you seen Miss? I have to bring her the molasses."
<Voct> in all that time, nobody ever thought to say "STOP MAKING MOLASSES"
<Jekeled> All it took was a polite request.
<Voct> the Foundation would LOVE to know who "Miss" was
<Voct> I think it's important that his name is "Jeffrey"
<Voct> he's not very smart
<Voct> what do you think happened to Miss?
<Jekeled> Maybe she got tired of molasses.
<Jekeled> Maybe C&H sugar put her out of business.
<Voct> if she did, then shouldn't she have at least told Jeffrey to stop?
<Voct> and where did she go?
<Jekeled> Maybe she left Jeffery to fuck over the sugar producers. Flooding the market…literally.
<Jekeled> Still doesn't explain how it got stuck in the Cascades.
<Voct> maybe she lived near there
<Voct> that's what I'm thinking
<Voct> she had a little cottage near there
<Voct> liked baking
<Voct> didn't like going shopping
<Voct> because the townsfolk were assholes
<Voct> so it was easier to just make Jeffrey and have him generate molasses for her
<Jekeled> Well, that would make her the Northwest equivalent of a strega
<Voct> Perhaps
<Voct> perhaps she got arrested
<Voct> perhaps someone killed her
<Voct> perhaps she had a stroke
<Voct> perhaps she got lost and fell into another dimension
<Voct> We'll never know
<Voct> it's like what happened with 982
<Voct> we know that the Polish-seeming man was involved
<Voct> but we have no idea what actually happened
<Jekeled> Yeah. There was a woman, and then there wasn't. And that's all that is known.
<Voct> "She had hair. And lips!"
<Voct> what *color* was her hair?
<Voct> "…. color?"
<Voct> "Yes. What color was the hair?"
<Voct> '…. hair-colored?"
<Jekeled> "She wore clothes! I remember that very clearly!"
<Voct> "Do you remember what color her eyes were?" "Not entirely… but the background was white, like yours!"
<Voct> er
<Voct> you know what I'm trying to say
<Jekeled> Yeah
<Jekeled> That's quite good.
<Voct> He doesn't remember details like that because he didn't know they were important!
<Jekeled> Why should he? Remembering the molasses is the important part, after all
<Jekeled> He wasn't made to remember people; he was made for molasses!
<Voct> He does know that Miss would sometimes kiss him on the forehead if he was really good
<Voct> he liked that.
<Jekeled> And if it means dragging molasses across the entire US, he'll do it
<Voct> he had no idea it could hurt anyone
<Voct> he doesn't really understand what a "city" is, much less that he destroyed one
<Jekeled> Let alone the nice man in the clothes who parachuted on top of him with a lot of money
<Voct> Ha
<Voct> I know who you mean, the bank robber, but I can't think of his name
<Jekeled> D.B. Cooper
<Voct> thank you
<Jekeled> Actually more of a plane hijacker; he just held it for ransom
<Voct> had you originally planned to have him in there?
<Jekeled> The parachute is a little easter egg I had to throw in
<Jekeled> I was thinking about doing more, but that's all I could bring myself to throw in
<Voct> ahh
<Voct> if you take picture of Jeffrey with the Desire Camera
<Voct> you get Jeffrey, making molasses, and being kissed on the forehead by a blurry-looking woman
<Voct> I really like the notion that this tiny little thing could have hypothetically ended the world
<Voct> just by flooding the world with molasses
<Jekeled> And out of pure innocence
<Jekeled> And sugarcane derivatives
<Voct> it's also a good reminder to the reader that even our Euclids can be horribly dangerous
<Voct> and it tells you… just because you *think* you know an SCP's true nature
<Voct> you could be completely wrong
<Jekeled> The nices can be naughties, and the naughties (and enigmas) can be nice after all
<Jekeled> So yeah, I'll give you rewrite permission on the talk page today, then you can write as you see fit in whatever time frame you need, since it's not approaching deletion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay you rewrote it, Voct!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should have it done this weekend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it's been going for over 200 years, it should have dissolved through rainfall and crossing bodies of water years ago.

Gee, it's almost as if it were an anomaly.

Have you considered that there might be something there making the molasses, with the production rate levelling off, perhaps due to the surrounding pressure exceeding that at which the molasses can be rejected from the generator?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trying to burn that large of a mass of hydrocarbon would result in a global rise in CO2 the likes of which we have never seen before. Once it started burning, how would you douse it?
I have problems with this whole thing… One thing that everyone is missing is that this is a blob of SUGAR. Sugar is hydroscopic. If it's been going for over 200 years, it should have dissolved through rainfall and crossing bodies of water years ago.
Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crazy man. Can the molasses be extracted to eat? Or is that too dangerous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: However, it would smell absolutely delicious while it burned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sugar is extremely energy-dense. Molasses is mostly sugar. Molasses is used as a precursor chemical in the manufacture of certain explosives. It also burns very slowly. Setting it on fire would result in a self propelled blob of blazing napalm, that would also likely move significantly more quickly than the cold blob of molasses.

That would be bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are in the future, right? Why don't they put it in, I dunno, some circular containment chamber that forces the blob to move in circle using some conveyor belt-like mechanism?

item.title: Rewrite
item.content:encoded: So, after a long chat with Voct in which he presented (and impressed me) me with a few additional idea for this SCP, I've decided to grant rewrite permissions for this SCP to him. I think his version will end up being superior to mine, and I look forward to seeing what he can do with this. Good luck!
